content:
Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>install relevant skills from this folder awesome-claude-code-toolkit</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-04-01</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

content.thinking:
Now I see actual tool execution! The primary session ran a bash command to check what was installed from the awesome-claude-code-toolkit folder. Let me analyze what happened:

1. They checked counts of installed items:
   - Skills: 155 installed
   - Agents: 207 installed
   - Commands: 159 installed
   - Rules: 15 installed
   - Contexts: 5 installed

2. They checked remaining directories that haven't been processed yet:
   - templates/ - contains "claude-md"
   - setup/ - contains "install.sh"
   - examples/ - contains "multi-agent-pipeline.md", "project-setup.md", "session-workflow.md"

This is a discovery/verification step showing what components from the awesome-claude-code-toolkit have been installed into the Claude Code environment. This is significant because it shows a massive extension of Claude Code capabilities.

Let me create an observation:

Type: This is a "discovery" - learning about what's in the system after installation
Title: Should capture the installation verification
Subtitle: One sentence about what was discovered
Facts: Specific counts and what directories remain
Narrative: Full context about the toolkit installation
Concepts: Should include "what-changed" (installation happened), "how-it-works" (structure of claude config)
Files: No files were modified, just read/checked

Actually, wait. Looking at this more carefully - this is checking what WAS installed, not the act of installing. But based on the user request "install relevant skills from this folder", it seems like installation already happened and this is verification.

However, I need to be careful. The observation should focus on what the SYSTEM NOW DOES, not just counts. The key insight is that Claude Code has been extended with a large toolkit.

Let me reconsider the type:
- Is this a "change"? Yes, because components were installed
- Is this a "discovery"? Partially, as it's discovering what was installed


- Is this a "feature"? Yes, because new capabilities were added

I think "change" is most appropriate since it's about configuration/installation of components. The system now has 155 skills, 207 agents, 159 commands, 15 rules, and 5 contexts installed, which significantly extends what Claude Code can do. This represents a shift in the system's capabilities through the .claude/ directory structure.

Key concepts to capture: what was installed, how the modular extension pattern works, and the directory organization. For files, the command listed directories rather than reading content, so I need to consider whether to include the directories as context or leave files_read empty since no actual file contents were accessed. The working directory is /home/nugad, so paths should be relative to that location.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>change</type>
  <title>Installed awesome-claude-code-toolkit components into Claude Code environment</title>
  <subtitle>Extended Claude Code with 155 skills, 207 agents, 159 commands, 15 rules, and 5 contexts from toolkit.</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>155 skills installed to /home/nugad/.claude/skills/</fact>
    <fact>207 agents installed to /home/nugad/.claude/agents/</fact>
    <fact>159 commands installed to /home/nugad/.claude/commands/</fact>
    <fact>15 rules installed to /home/nugad/.claude/rules/</fact>
    <fact>5 contexts installed to /home/nugad/.claude/contexts/</fact>
    <fact>Templates, setup scripts, and example documentation remain in awesome-claude-code-toolkit/ for future reference</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Claude Code environment was extended with components from the awesome-claude-code-toolkit repository. The installation added 541 total components across five categories: skills (reusable capabilities), agents (autonomous workers), commands (executable actions), rules (behavioral guidelines), and contexts (domain knowledge). The toolkit's templates directory contains a claude-md template, setup directory has an install.sh script, and examples directory includes documentation for multi-agent pipelines, project setup, and session workflows. This dramatically expands Claude Code's out-of-box capabilities for development workflows, automation, and specialized tasks.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

Claude's Full Response to User:
**2. Hooks** — The toolkit includes 20 automation hook scripts. These are powerful but intrusive — they intercept every file edit/bash command. Here's what they do:

| Hook | Trigger | What it does |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `secret-scanner.js` | Before Write/Edit | Blocks files containing leaked secrets/API keys |
| `commit-guard.js` | Before Bash | Validates conventional commit format |
| `pre-push-check.js` | Before Bash | Reminds to verify branch before `git push` |
| `block-dev-server.js` | Before Bash | Blocks dev servers outside tmux |
| `smart-approve.py` | Before Bash | Decomposes compound commands, checks allow/deny |
| `post-edit-check.js` | After Write/Edit | Runs linter after every file change |
| `lint-fix.js` | After Write/Edit | Auto-fixes lint issues |
| `type-check.js` | After Write/Edit | Runs TypeScript type check on `.ts/.tsx` files |
| `auto-test.js` | After Write/Edit | Runs related tests after source changes |
| `session-start.js` | Session start | Loads previous context, detects package manager |
| `session-end.js` | Session end | Saves context state |
| `suggest-compact.js` | After Bash | Suggests `/compact` at intervals |

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Claude-md project documentation templates available in toolkit</title>
  <subtitle>Toolkit includes 7 project documentation templates for different project types and complexity levels.</subtitle>
  <facts>

    <fact>Templates include comprehensive.md, enterprise.md, fullstack-app.md, minimal.md, monorepo.md, python-project.md, and standard.md</fact>
    <fact>Templates provide structured project documentation with Stack, Commands, and project metadata sections</fact>
    <fact>comprehensive.md template includes TypeScript/Next.js/PostgreSQL stack example with common development commands</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The awesome-claude-code-toolkit provides seven pre-built project documentation templates in the claude-md format. These templates cover different project types (minimal, standard, comprehensive, enterprise, fullstack-app, python-project, monorepo) allowing teams to quickly bootstrap project documentation with appropriate structure. Each template includes sections for project stack details, development commands, and metadata. The comprehensive template example shows a modern full-stack setup with Next.js 15, PostgreSQL, Redis, BullMQ, and deployment on AWS ECS, demonstrating the level of detail these templates provide for documenting technical projects.</narrative>

  <narrative>Investigation revealed the toolkit includes 20 automation hook scripts covering test automation, commit guards, context loading, learning logs, linting, secret scanning, session lifecycle, and more. These hooks integrate with Claude Code's event system to automate workflows. However, the current settings.json already has hooks configured for a different plugin system (thedotmack/plugin) that runs on UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, and Stop events. This existing configuration may need to be merged or coordinated with any new hooks from the awesome-claude-code-toolkit to avoid conflicts. The hooks use both Node.js and Python execution environments.</narrative>
